findings.description: Mixes typing.Optional with PEP 585 builtin generics (dict[str, ...]); inconsistent annotation style could be unified.
Module-level logger is created but never used anywhere, so no cache event (hit, miss, eviction, error) is ever logged.
RedisClient.setex silently ignores ttl_seconds so L2 entries never expire, and combined with L1 re-promotion resetting timestamps stale data can persist indefinitely.
L1 dict is unbounded with no eviction policy and expired entries are purged only when individually accessed, causing unbounded memory growth in long-running processes.

_is_expired comparison is inverted (age < self.ttl returns True for fresh entries), so fresh L1 entries are evicted on every get while entries older than the TTL are reported fresh and served stale indefinitely.
Non-atomic check-then-del on _local (and no locking anywhere in the class) can raise KeyError or lose updates when the cache is shared across threads; dict.pop(key, None) would be safe.

pickle.loads on bytes fetched from Redis allows arbitrary code execution if the Redis keyspace is shared or poisoned (CWE-502); a data-only serializer such as JSON or msgpack is safer for cache values.
L1 is written before the L2 write, so if pickle.dumps or redis.setex raises the tiers diverge (this process serves a value other processes never see) and no error handling exists.

stats.local_entries is updated only in set(), never on get-path expiry deletion, L2 promotion, or invalidate, and export_stats bypasses it with len(self._local), leaving the field stale and redundant.
None is used as the miss sentinel, so a legitimately cached None is indistinguishable from a miss and get_or_compute re-invokes compute() on every call when the computed value is None.
get_or_compute has no stampede protection, so concurrent misses on the same key each invoke compute() redundantly (thundering herd on expensive computations).
invalidate clears only this process's L1 and Redis; other instances' local caches keep serving the stale value until their TTL elapses because there is no cross-process invalidation mechanism.
